one_line_takeaway: By comparing RIPPER rule sets learned before and after MAB-Malware-driven adversarial evasion, the authors detect and feature-level-explain concept drift in six malware families with 92.08% overall accuracy, though detection reliability varies sharply by family (100% for Agensla/Mokes vs. 22.5% error for DCRat).
key_finding: Across all six families and all eight dimensionalities, overall concept-drift detection accuracy was 92.08%. Per-family behavior (from Table 2, errors out of 10 runs per dimensionality): Agensla and Mokes were detected with 100% accuracy at every dimensionality (0 errors in all 8x10 runs each). Taskun had only 1 error out of 80 runs, occurring at dimensionality 100. DCRat had the worst performance, a 22.5% error rate aggregated across all dimensionalities, though its error rate dropped to 0% specifically at dimensionality 25. Makoob showed the smallest gap between within-family and cross-family rule distances (the hardest case to separate), while Mokes showed the largest gap, with within-family distance up to 15 times smaller than the original-vs-adversarial distance across all tested dimensionalities.
core_contribution: The paper (Jurečková and Jureček, CTU Prague) proposes an interpretable concept-drift detector for malware family evolution. Instead of using a black-box classifier to flag drift, they train a RIPPER rule-based classifier (via the 'wittgenstein' Python library) on features of a malware family, generate a second rule set from an 'evolved' version of the family, and compute the normalized Hamming distance between the binary detection vectors produced by the two rule sets over a held-out sample set. A drift alarm fires when this rule-set distance exceeds a decision threshold defined as the arithmetic mean of (a) the average within-family distance (rules learned from two random splits of the same original family) and (b) the average original-vs-adversarial distance. Because RIPPER rules are explicit conjunctions of feature conditions, the same comparison also indicates which specific PE-file features/values changed between the original and evolved family, giving a feature-level explanation of the drift, not just a drift score.
threat_model: The 'evolution' of each malware family is simulated by running MAB-Malware (Song et al. 2020), a reinforcement-learning/multi-armed-bandit adversarial malware generator, against an EMBER-based PE malware classifier (Anderson & Roth, 2018). MAB-Malware applies functionality-preserving binary manipulations (appending benign overlay/section data, adding/renaming sections, zeroing certificate/debug fields, corrupting optional header checksums, semantics-preserving code transforms) to make each family's samples evade the EMBER classifier; only samples that successfully evaded EMBER were kept as the 'adversarial'/evolved family. The paper's own threat model is thus: an attacker mutates a known malware family via automated evasion tooling, and a defender wants to (1) notice that the family's statistical/structural signature has drifted and (2) understand, in human-readable terms, which PE features changed. The paper explicitly notes a boundary condition: the method assumes samples have already been correctly assigned to a known family by an upstream classifier; it does not detect drift for entirely new/zero-day families (for which the authors point to their own prior work, Jurečková et al. 2024, as the applicable mechanism, not as part of this paper's contribution).
limitations: The paper explicitly states these boundaries: (1) the method presupposes correct family assignment already exists (an upstream classifier has labeled the test sample's family) and does not handle zero-day/unknown families -- for those, drift is simply not detected under this framework; (2) 'evolution' in the experiments is entirely synthetic, generated by a single adversarial tool (MAB-Malware) against a single target classifier (EMBER), not observed real-world temporal drift, so it is unclear whether the same rule-distance signal generalizes to organic, non-adversarially-generated family evolution over time; (3) DCRat's substantially higher error rate (22.5%) versus near-perfect detection for Agensla/Mokes/Taskun is reported but not diagnosed -- the paper does not analyze why some families are structurally harder to separate with rule-distance thresholds; (4) only one distance metric (normalized Hamming distance on rule-coverage vectors) and one rule learner (RIPPER) were evaluated; the authors list trying decision trees and other distance metrics as future work rather than something they tested.

datasets_used: RawMal-TF (Bálik, Jureček, Stamp 2025) -- source of original malware family binaries
EMBER (Anderson & Roth, 2018) -- source of benign feature vectors for RIPPER training and target classifier attacked by MAB-Malware
quantitative_results: Overall concept-drift detection accuracy across all 6 families and 8 dimensionalities: 92.08%
Agensla: 0 errors out of 10 runs at every dimensionality (3,5,10,15,25,50,75,100) -- 100% accuracy
Mokes: 0 errors out of 10 runs at every dimensionality -- 100% accuracy
Taskun: 1 error out of 80 total runs (occurred at dimensionality 100)
DCRat: 22.5% error rate aggregated across all dimensionalities; 0% error rate specifically at dimensionality 25
Mokes family: within-family rule distance up to 15x smaller than original-vs-adversarial rule distance across all dimensionalities (largest separation of any family)
Makoob family: smallest gap between within-family and cross-family rule distances (hardest to detect)
Sample counts (original/adversarial): Agensla 8,418/2,558; DCRat 1,026/1,010; Makoob 2,414/626; Mokes 2,216/2,058; Strab 2,191/1,596; Taskun 4,888/1,015
baselines_compared: No direct experimental baseline comparison is performed in this paper -- Transcend (Jordaney et al. 2017), ADClust (Kwon & Kang 2020), and the drift-tracking method of Singh et al. 2012 are discussed only in Related Work as prior black-box or non-explanatory drift detectors, not re-implemented or benchmarked against the proposed method

Within-family (set1 vs set2) rule distance serves as the paper's own internal control/baseline against which cross-family (original vs. adversarial) distance is compared to define the decision threshold
